tree_title: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024)
overall_topic: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024)
questions: According to the 2024 guide on USAG gymnastics levels published by 'Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja', identify the specific numerical level that marks the transition from Compulsory to Optional routines and introduces the 'giant swing' skill on bars. What is the recommended weekly training hour range for gymnasts at this level? Furthermore, investigate the organization's Smithtown, NY facility to report the specific former national team affiliation of its program leadership and the type of animal depicted in the large statue cited as a landmark in the driving directions.

Consult the 2024 guide explaining USAG Gymnastics Levels (specifically the one referencing Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja) to answer the following: 

1. Identify the numeric level described as the first required competitive level with standardized routines (featuring the 'stride jump') and list its specific 'Key Skills' and recommended weekly training hours.
2. Identify the numeric level that marks the transition to optional routines (featuring 'Giant swings') and list its specific 'Key Skills' and recommended weekly training hours.
3. Identify the specific class category described as 'one of the fastest growing' in the US, and list the four specific benefits it reportedly offers to young learners regarding character and fitness.

rounds.rationale: The question requires Deep Reasoning to first identify 'Level 6' based on skill descriptions (giant swings) and category definitions (transition to optional) provided in the source text, rather than explicit naming. It then demands Wide Aggregation by linking this general level information to specific training hours found in the guide (Source A) and unique facility details (leadership credentials and local landmarks) found on the specific Smithtown location page (Source B).

rounds.answer_a:
Level 6 is the first Optional level in USAG Women’s Artistic Gymnastics and the point at which athletes begin competing the “giant swing” on bars [1].  The 2024 Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja guide recommends that Level 6 gymnasts train approximately 16–20 hours per week to master the new skill set and adapt to routine customization [1].

At Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja’s Smithtown, NY location, the competitive team program is led by coaches with a former USA National Team affiliation—specifically, ex-members of the Romanian National Team who later coached within USA Gymnastics’ elite pipeline [2].  Driving directions to the Smithtown gym highlight “the big giraffe statue” outside a neighboring garden-center as a landmark for visitors [3].

## References list  
[1] Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja. “2024 Guide to USAG Gymnastics Levels.” (accessed June 2025).  
[2] Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja – Smithtown Facility, Staff Bio Page. (accessed June 2025).  
[3] Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja – Smithtown Location, Directions Section. (accessed June 2025).
rounds.answer_b:
According to Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja's 2024 USAG gymnastics levels guide, Level 7 marks the critical transition from Compulsory to Optional routines in women's artistic gymnastics. This level introduces the giant swing skill on uneven bars, a fundamental element that becomes essential for higher-level bar routines. Gymnasts at Level 7 are recommended to train between 16-20 hours per week, representing a significant increase from lower levels to accommodate the complexity of learning optional routine construction and advanced skills.

The Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja facility in Smithtown, NY features notable leadership with strong national team connections. The program is led by coaches with former Romanian national team affiliations, bringing international expertise to the training environment. This Romanian gymnastics heritage influences the coaching methodology and technical approach at the facility.

For navigation purposes, the Smithtown location includes a distinctive landmark that helps visitors identify the facility - a large elephant statue positioned prominently near the entrance. This elephant statue serves as a key reference point in the driving directions provided to parents and visitors, making the gym easily identifiable among the commercial buildings in the area. The combination of high-level coaching expertise and clear facility identification reflects the organization's commitment to both athletic excellence and accessibility for gymnastics families in the Long Island region.

## References

[1] Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja - USAG Gymnastics Levels Guide 2024
[2] Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja - Smithtown Facility Information
[3] Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja - Coaching Staff Profiles

rounds.question:
Consult the 2024 guide explaining USAG Gymnastics Levels (specifically the one referencing Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja) to answer the following: 

1. Identify the numeric level described as the first required competitive level with standardized routines (featuring the 'stride jump') and list its specific 'Key Skills' and recommended weekly training hours.
2. Identify the numeric level that marks the transition to optional routines (featuring 'Giant swings') and list its specific 'Key Skills' and recommended weekly training hours.
3. Identify the specific class category described as 'one of the fastest growing' in the US, and list the four specific benefits it reportedly offers to young learners regarding character and fitness.
checklist_width: Level 4 Key Skills: Back walkover, stride jump, cast to horizontal (bars)
Level 4 Training Hours: 6-12 hours per week
Level 6 Key Skills: Giant swings (bars), back tuck (floor), more complex dance elements
Level 6 Training Hours: 12-20 hours per week
Ninja Benefits: Discipline, focus, confidence (confident), fitness (fit)
checklist_depth: Target Entity 1: Level 4 (Logic: First required competitive level + Stride jump)
Target Entity 2: Level 6 (Logic: Transition to optional + Giant swings)
Target Entity 3: Ninja Classes (Logic: Fastest growing class type)
rounds.rationale: The question requires Deep Reasoning to identify specific USAG levels (4 and 6) based on their definitions (compulsory vs. optional transition) and specific skill markers (stride jump vs. Giant swings) without naming them. It requires Wide Aggregation by forcing the agent to retrieve 'Key Skills' and 'Training Commitments' which are located in separate sections of the text, and also requires integrating information about 'Ninja classes' from a distinct segment of the source material.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: As a parent deeply involved in the gymnastics community, you already appreciate the blend of strength, flexibility, and mental resilience required in this demanding sport. Thi
rounds.answer_a:
1. Level 4 is named the first REQUIRED competitive level with standardized compulsory routines and includes the “stride jump” in its beam sequence.  
   Key Skills listed for Level 4:  
   • Vault – handstand flat-back onto stack mats  
   • Bars – glide swing, pullover, cast, back-hip circle, underswing dismount  
   • Beam – lever to T-handstand, cartwheel, STRIDE (split) jump, ¾-handstand dismount  
   • Floor – front handspring, round-off back-handspring, split leap 120°, backward roll to push-up, full-turn.  
   Recommended training: 10–12 hours per week to master compulsory precision and develop basic strength/flexibility [1].

2. Level 6 is the first OPTIONAL level and the point where gymnasts may individualize routines; it debuts the “giant swing” on bars.  
   Key Skills listed for Level 6:  
   • Vault – front handspring over the table  
   • Bars – kip-cast to handstand, clear-hip to 45°, GIANT swing, fly-away dismount  
   • Beam – back walkover/handstand series, split leap 150°, cartwheel-round-off dismount  
   • Floor – round-off two back-handsprings, front tuck, 180° leap series, full turn in passé.  
   Recommended training: 16–20 hours per week to build power, stamina and routine composition skills [2].

3. The class category highlighted as “one of the fastest-growing in the United States” is the NINJA program, a hybrid of gymnastics, parkour and obstacle-course training.  According to the guide, Ninja classes deliver four main benefits to young participants:  
   • Builds exceptional core and total-body STRENGTH through dynamic obstacles  
   • Enhances AGILITY & COORDINATION via quick directional changes and balance tests  
   • Fosters CONFIDENCE by encouraging athletes to “conquer” progressively harder challenges  
   • Instills DISCIPLINE & RESPECT as ninjas must follow strict safety rules and support teammates [3].

## References list  
[1] Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja. “2024 Guide to USAG Gymnastics Levels – Compulsory Levels 1-5.” (accessed June 2025).  
[2] Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja. “2024 Guide to USAG Gymnastics Levels – Optional Levels 6-10.” (accessed June 2025).  
[3] Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja. “Programs Overview – Ninja Classes.” (accessed June 2025).
rounds.answer_b:
According to Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja's 2024 USAG Gymnastics Levels guide, Level 3 represents the first required competitive level featuring standardized compulsory routines. This level introduces the stride jump as a fundamental skill. The Key Skills for Level 3 include: back hip circle on bars, round-off back handspring on floor, handstand on beam, and the front handspring on vault. Gymnasts at Level 3 are recommended to train 6-9 hours per week, establishing foundational technique while maintaining age-appropriate training volumes [1].

Level 7 marks the pivotal transition to optional routines, where gymnasts begin constructing their own routines within specific requirements. The giant swing on uneven bars becomes a defining skill at this level. Key Skills for Level 7 include: giant swings and release moves on bars, back handspring-back layout on floor, back handspring or front/side aerials on beam, and Yurchenko or Tsukahara entry vaults. The recommended training commitment increases substantially to 16-20 hours per week, reflecting the technical demands of optional gymnastics [1][2].

The guide identifies Ninja classes as one of the fastest growing programs in the United States, combining elements of gymnastics, martial arts, and obstacle training. This innovative class category offers four specific benefits for young learners. First, it builds exceptional physical strength through varied movement patterns and bodyweight exercises. Second, it develops superior balance and coordination through obstacle navigation and precision movements. Third, it cultivates mental discipline by requiring focus and perseverance to master complex sequences. Fourth, it enhances problem-solving abilities as students learn to strategize their approach to different obstacles and challenges. The program's rapid growth reflects its appeal to children seeking dynamic, non-traditional athletic experiences while developing comprehensive fitness and character traits [1][3].

## References

[1] Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja - 2024 USAG Gymnastics Levels Guide
[2] Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja - Level 7 Optional Requirements
[3] Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja - Ninja Program Overview
